articles.title: How Diet & The Gut Microbiome Intersect to Boost Cancer
articles.summary_4_sentences: The article describes evidence linking dietary patterns to changes in the gut microbiome that can influence colorectal cancer progression. It reports that high-fiber diets promote beneficial bacteria that produce short-chain fatty acids with anti-tumor properties. It also reports that diets rich in red meat foster microbial metabolites that may enhance tumor growth. The piece frames diet-microbiome interactions as a potential avenue for prevention strategies.

articles.title: Vaping Linked to Oral and Lung Cancers
articles.summary_4_sentences: This report links vaping with increased risks of oral and lung cancers. It cites epidemiological data showing higher incidence rates among people who vape. Researchers discuss possible carcinogenic compounds present in e-cigarette aerosols as a contributing factor. The article calls for public health interventions to mitigate vaping-related cancer risks.

articles.title: How aggressive breast cancer turns off the immune system
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers describe a project focused on aggressive breast cancer and the challenge of predicting disease progression. The work centers on how tumors interact with and suppress the immune system. Using patient samples, the team aims to identify biomarkers that reflect how the cancer evolves. The stated goal is to translate earlier findings into clinical tools that support more personalized treatment.

articles.title: A common nutrient could supercharge cancer treatment
articles.summary_4_sentences: The article reports that zeaxanthin, a nutrient commonly associated with eye health, may strengthen T cells in a way that enhances immunotherapy impact. It notes that the compound is found in vegetables and supplements and is described as safe and accessible. The findings are presented as support for using the nutrient as a cancer-therapy booster. The report states that human trials are the next step.

articles.title: Scientists finally uncover why promising cancer drugs keep failing
articles.summary_4_sentences: The article discusses why BET inhibitors have often underperformed in patients despite early promise. It reports new research indicating that BRD2 and BRD4, two related proteins targeted by these drugs, have distinct roles rather than redundant functions. BRD2 is described as preparing genes for activation, while BRD4 drives the final activation step. The piece suggests that simultaneously blocking both proteins may disrupt gene regulation in ways that undermine therapeutic benefit.

articles.title: Goodbye colonoscopy? New stool test detects 90% of colorectal cancers
articles.summary_4_sentences: The article describes an AI-driven approach that analyzes gut bacteria patterns in stool samples to detect colorectal cancer. It reports that the method identified 90% of colorectal cancer cases in the study described. The work relies on mapping gut bacteria at high resolution to find microbial signatures linked to cancer. The piece suggests this could provide a less invasive alternative or complement to colonoscopy-based detection.

articles.title: Scientists discover hidden gut signals that could detect cancer early
articles.summary_4_sentences: This study report says gut bacteria and metabolites may provide biomarkers that enable earlier detection of serious digestive diseases. Using AI, researchers found that biomarkers tied to one condition can help predict others. The findings are presented as evidence that these digestive diseases are interconnected at the biomarker level. The article suggests such cross-disease signals could speed diagnosis and reduce reliance on invasive procedures.

articles.title: Scientists create “smart” DNA drug that targets cancer cells with extreme precision
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers report a programmable therapeutic system built from synthetic DNA designed to activate only in the presence of a specific combination of tumor markers. The article says this conditional activation is intended to reduce damage to healthy tissue. It also describes the system as capable of delivering multiple drugs at once. The work is presented as a step toward responsive, machine-like medicines inside the body.

articles.title: What’s hiding inside colon cancer could change treatment
articles.summary_4_sentences: The report states that colorectal tumors may consistently host distinct microbial communities compared with other cancers. It describes an analysis of DNA from more than 9,000 patients used to evaluate tumor-associated microbes. The findings are presented as challenging the idea that all cancers have their own consistent microbial signatures. The article suggests the colorectal cancer microbial "fingerprint" could influence future diagnosis and treatment approaches.

articles.title: How APOE4 Disrupts the Brain Before Memory Loss
articles.summary_4_sentences: Scientists examine how the APOE4 allele affects brain function before measurable memory decline. The study described reports early synaptic and metabolic disturbances in APOE4 carriers. These findings are framed as evidence that APOE4 accelerates neurodegenerative processes. The article suggests the work may inform earlier intervention strategies for Alzheimer's disease.

articles.title: Your nose could detect Alzheimer’s years before symptoms begin
articles.summary_4_sentences: The article reports that loss of smell may be an early indicator of Alzheimer's disease. It describes findings that immune cells in the brain destroy smell-related nerve fibers after detecting abnormal signals on their surfaces. The reported damage begins in early stages of disease before cognitive decline. The work is presented as potentially enabling earlier identification of at-risk patients and improved timing of treatment.

articles.title: Scientists say we’ve been treating Alzheimer’s all wrong
articles.summary_4_sentences: This article argues that Alzheimer's should be viewed as a complex mix of biology, aging, and overall health rather than a single-problem disease. It says single-target drugs have produced limited results, even when benefits exist. The piece describes interest in multi-pronged strategies including gene editing, brain-cell rejuvenation approaches, and gut health interventions. The stated aim is to address Alzheimer's as a system rather than focusing on one factor at a time.

articles.title: Scientists just found a hidden “drain” inside the human brain
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers report imaging evidence for a waste-removal pathway in the human brain. Using MRI, they observed fluid flow along the middle meningeal artery in a slow, lymphatic-like pattern distinct from blood flow. The article describes this as confirmation of a previously unknown drainage hub. It suggests the finding could affect how scientists study brain aging, injury, and diseases including Alzheimer's.

articles.title: Brain study reveals hidden link between autism and ADHD
articles.summary_4_sentences: The article reports research suggesting autism-like trait severity, rather than diagnostic labels, relates to differences in brain network wiring. It describes findings that brain networks involved in thinking and social behavior remain unusually connected in children with stronger autism symptoms. The report notes this pattern appears even in children without an official autism diagnosis. The findings are framed as pointing to a distinct developmental trajectory that may help explain overlap with ADHD.

articles.title: Seeing and imagining activate some of the same brain cells
articles.summary_4_sentences: Neuroimaging experiments compared brain activity during visual perception with activity during mental imagery. The results showed overlapping activation in regions of the visual cortex across both conditions. The overlap is interpreted as evidence for shared mechanisms supporting seeing and imagining. The study is presented as advancing understanding of how the brain builds internal representations.

articles.title: This “rotten egg” brain gas could be the key to fighting Alzheimer’s disease
articles.summary_4_sentences: The article reports on experiments implicating a protein called CSE that helps produce small amounts of hydrogen sulfide in the brain. In genetically engineered mice, removing CSE was associated with memory loss and brain damage along with Alzheimer’s-like hallmarks, including weakened blood-brain barriers and reduced neurogenesis. The findings are framed as suggesting that carefully regulated hydrogen sulfide may protect brain cells and support memory. The report positions this pathway as a potential new direction for Alzheimer’s research.

articles.title: Scientists map the brain’s hidden wiring using RNA barcodes in major breakthrough
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers report a method that uses RNA barcodes to map neuronal connections with single-synapse precision. The approach is described as converting circuit mapping into a sequencing task, making it faster and more scalable than traditional mapping. In mice, it revealed connections described as previously unknown. The article suggests the technique could support earlier detection and more targeted treatment of neurological diseases.

articles.title: Scientists just watched Alzheimer’s damage happen in real time
articles.summary_4_sentences: Scientists report observing real-time chemical interactions implicated in Alzheimer's disease. The work focuses on how metal ions, especially copper, can trigger harmful protein clumping in the brain. The findings are presented as clarifying a molecular-level picture of disease development. The article frames the work as addressing a long-standing challenge of directly observing damaging processes as they occur.

articles.title: Your vitamin D levels in midlife could shape your brain decades later
articles.summary_4_sentences: The article reports a long-term study of nearly 800 people followed for 16 years. It states that higher vitamin D levels in participants' 30s and 40s were linked to lower tau protein levels later in life. Tau is described as a marker associated with dementia. The findings are presented as suggesting midlife vitamin D status may influence later brain health.

topic_groups.topic: Infectious Disease, Vaccines, and Immune Mechanisms

articles.title: Hidden weak spots in HIV and Ebola revealed with breakthrough nanodisc technology
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers describe a nanodisc-based platform that studies viral proteins in a form that more closely mimics real viruses. The approach is reported to reveal antibody interactions that traditional methods missed for viruses including HIV and Ebola. By recreating a membrane-like environment, the platform aims to improve understanding of immune recognition. The article suggests the technique could speed development of more effective vaccines.

articles.title: Scientists finally crack mystery of rare COVID vaccine blood clots
articles.summary_4_sentences: The report describes research into a rare blood-clotting disorder associated with some COVID-19 vaccines or adenovirus infections. It states that the immune system can mistakenly target the blood protein PF4 after confusing it with a viral protein. This immune response is described as triggering clotting in extremely rare cases. The article says the findings could allow vaccines to be redesigned to avoid this reaction while maintaining effectiveness.

articles.title: Are Autoantibodies to Blame for Long COVID?
articles.summary_4_sentences: The article examines whether autoantibodies contribute to prolonged symptoms after COVID-19. It reports elevated autoantibody levels in long-COVID patients. Researchers propose that autoimmune mechanisms may underlie persistent fatigue and neurological problems described in long COVID. The piece highlights autoantibody-related pathways as potential therapeutic targets for managing long COVID.

articles.title: A drug already in trials may finally stop hepatitis E
articles.summary_4_sentences: The article reports that bemnifosbuvir, a drug in trials for hepatitis C, may block hepatitis E virus replication. It states that the drug disrupts the virus's genetic machinery in tests. The report describes effectiveness in cell and animal studies without harming healthy tissue. It suggests the drug could be repurposed for hepatitis E if ongoing trials are successful.

articles.title: Scientists discover why flu and COVID hit older adults so hard
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers report that aging lungs may drive increased severity of flu and COVID-19 in older adults. They describe certain lung cells that can trigger an exaggerated immune response, forming clusters of inflammatory cells that damage tissue. The article notes experiments in which activating an aging-related signal in young mice made their lungs behave like older ones during illness. The findings are presented as clarifying why immune protection can become damaging with age.

articles.title: Amid vaccine policy whiplash, here’s how a pediatrician talks to families
articles.summary_4_sentences: A pediatrician describes approaches to discussing vaccines with families amid changing policies. The article emphasizes building trust, addressing concerns, and presenting evidence clearly. It also notes the effect of recent court rulings on vaccine access. The piece argues that effective communication is crucial to maintaining immunization rates.

topic_groups.topic: Metabolism, Obesity, Nutrition, and Cardiovascular Risk

articles.title: Why Ozempic doesn’t work for everyone: Scientists just found a hidden reason
articles.summary_4_sentences: The article reports that GLP-1 drugs such as Ozempic and Wegovy may be less effective for about 10% of people due to genetic variants. It describes this as a form of "GLP-1 resistance" in which individuals have higher levels of the targeted hormone but do not respond properly. The report frames this as a hidden biological reason for variable weight-loss and diabetes-drug responses. It suggests genetic factors could help explain who benefits most from these medications.

articles.title: Two simple eating habits linked to lower weight, study finds
articles.summary_4_sentences: A study reported here links meal timing to later body weight outcomes. People who fasted longer overnight and ate an early breakfast were more likely to have a lower BMI years later. The article suggests this may relate to aligning eating patterns with the body's internal clock. It also reports that skipping breakfast as intermittent fasting did not show the same advantage and may be associated with unhealthy habits.

articles.title: Early weight gain is linked to lifelong health consequences
articles.summary_4_sentences: The article reports that obesity in early adulthood is associated with a higher risk of premature death. It notes increased risk particularly from major diseases such as heart disease and diabetes. The report suggests that carrying excess weight for longer periods may increase cumulative damage. It also states that cancer risk in women did not follow the same pattern in this analysis, implying additional biological factors.

articles.title: BMI Not Useful for About One Third of People
articles.summary_4_sentences: This report states that BMI misclassifies about one-third of individuals in terms of health risk. It argues that this misclassification can obscure true metabolic health. The article highlights limitations of using BMI as a sole indicator. It recommends incorporating additional measures such as waist circumference for better assessment.

articles.title: Simple Japanese Eating Habit Promotes Longevity
articles.summary_4_sentences: The article examines the practice of "hara hachi bu," described as eating until 80% full. It reports an association between this habit and a lower incidence of age-related diseases. The piece states that modest caloric restriction may improve metabolic markers and promote cellular repair mechanisms. The findings are presented as support for portion control as a simple longevity-related intervention.

articles.title: The hidden tradeoff behind today’s most popular weight loss drugs
articles.summary_4_sentences: The article reports that weight loss drugs and bariatric surgery lead to similar changes in body composition. Both are described as reducing fat while also causing a modest loss of muscle. It notes that muscle mass is important for health outcomes beyond body weight alone. The piece frames these interventions as beneficial but involving trade-offs that should be considered.

articles.title: Scientists reveal new blood pressure treatment that works when others fail
articles.summary_4_sentences: The article reports results for a pill called baxdrostat in people with uncontrolled high blood pressure despite standard treatments. In a global trial described, patients experienced an average drop in blood pressure of nearly 10 mmHg. The report states the drug works by targeting a hormone involved in salt and water retention. The findings are presented as a potential new option for patients who do not respond to existing therapies.

articles.title: Binge drinking just once a month may triple your risk of liver scarring
articles.summary_4_sentences: The article reports that occasional binge drinking may significantly increase liver scarring risk in people with metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD). It cites a large U.S. study and highlights that MASLD affects about one in three adults. The reported association indicates that heavy drinking even once a month can raise the risk of serious scarring. The piece challenges the idea that infrequent binge drinking is harmless when overall drinking is otherwise moderate.

articles.title: Digital heart twins can guide a lifesaving procedure
articles.summary_4_sentences: Cardiologists created patient-specific digital heart models to simulate catheter ablation procedures. The simulations are described as predicting optimal ablation sites, reducing procedure time and complications. The article reports that clinical trials showed improved outcomes for patients with arrhythmias. The technology is presented as a step toward personalized cardiac interventions.

topic_groups.topic: Cannabis Use, Health Effects, and Policy

articles.title: The Link Between Cannabis, Depression, and Anxiety
articles.summary_4_sentences: A McMaster University study examined associations between cannabis use and mood disorders. The article states that parallels were observed with depression and anxiety. The report frames the findings as evidence of a relationship between cannabis use patterns and mental health outcomes. It does not provide intervention results in the provided summary.

articles.title: Study Highlights Parallels Between Cannabis Use and Alcohol Use Patterns Among Teens
articles.summary_4_sentences: Research published in Addiction is reported to analyze cannabis use among Swedish adolescents. The article states that cannabis use follows similar patterns to alcohol use in this population. It frames these similarities as relevant to understanding teen substance use behaviors. The summary does not specify causal mechanisms or intervention outcomes.

articles.title: Does Cannabis Enhance Sleep Quality?
articles.summary_4_sentences: A study in the Journal of Cannabis Research examined medical cannabis effects on sleep architecture. The trial reported longer total sleep time and fewer awakenings among participants using cannabis. Subjective sleep quality scores were reported to improve compared with baseline. The authors concluded cannabis may be a viable option for improving sleep quality in patients with insomnia.

articles.title: Scientists Develop 3D‑Printed Marijuana Breathalyzer
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers developed a portable breathalyzer intended to detect recent marijuana use. The device uses a 3D-printed sensor array designed to bind THC metabolites. Laboratory validation studies are reported to show high sensitivity and specificity. The article suggests potential use in law-enforcement and workplace testing for cannabis impairment.

articles.title: Who is Most Likely to Vape Cannabis?
articles.summary_4_sentences: A survey study identified predictors of cannabis vaping among adolescents. The report states higher risk among male, non-Hispanic White teens and among those who also use alcohol or tobacco. It also reports that vaping frequency correlated with increased exposure to THC. The article suggests these findings can inform targeted youth prevention strategies.

articles.title: Does Cannabis Legalization Contribute to Lower Crime Rates?
articles.summary_4_sentences: The article reports on a study analyzing crime statistics across jurisdictions after cannabis legalization. It states the findings suggest a correlation between legalization and reduced crime incidents. The piece discusses potential policy implications of this association. It also recommends additional longitudinal research to better assess the relationship.

articles.title: Study Indicates An Association Between Medical Cannabis Use and Decreases in Anxiety
articles.summary_4_sentences: A study published in Nature is reported to find an association between legal medical cannabis use and reduced anxiety levels. The article frames the result as observational in nature as presented in the summary. It does not specify dosing, product composition, or trial design in the provided text. The report suggests the association is relevant to how medical cannabis products are used for anxiety.

topic_groups.topic: Genetics, Gene Regulation, and Gene Therapy

articles.title: Overcoming the Hurdles that DNA Shape Creates in Gene Therapy
articles.summary_4_sentences: The article explains that CRISPR editing uses guide RNAs to target specific DNA sequences for genome modification. It highlights that the three-dimensional shape of DNA can impede the efficiency of gene therapy approaches. The report describes research showing that DNA structure influences binding of CRISPR components and can create hurdles for precise editing. It suggests understanding these structural barriers could improve the design of gene therapy vectors.

articles.title: Novel ALS Variants ID'ed, Now 1 in 4 Patients Have Genetic Cause
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers identified new genetic variants associated with ALS. The article states that known genetic causes now account for about 25% of ALS patients. It frames this as improving understanding of disease mechanisms and enabling better patient stratification. The report suggests the findings may improve diagnostic and therapeutic approaches for ALS.

articles.title: Your DNA has a secret “second code” that decides which genes get silenced
articles.summary_4_sentences: The article reports that cells can distinguish between genetic instructions that appear equivalent but differ in efficiency. It states that less efficient instructions can be selectively silenced. A protein called DHX29 is described as identifying and suppressing weaker messages. The findings are presented as revealing a hidden layer of gene-use control.

articles.title: Cracking the Code of a Crucial Class of Enzymes - E3 Ligases
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers investigated how E3 ligases function in ubiquitination. The report describes work that deciphers structural features determining substrate specificity. It frames the results as advancing understanding of protein turnover regulation. The article suggests these insights may support drug discovery efforts targeting ubiquitin-related pathways.

articles.title: Deafness reversed: One injection restores hearing in just weeks
articles.summary_4_sentences: The article reports a small gene therapy study in which a working copy of a hearing-related gene was delivered into the inner ear via a single injection. It states that all ten participants, ranging from children to adults, experienced improved hearing. Some participants reportedly showed gains within one month. The work is presented as evidence that gene therapy may help certain forms of inherited deafness.

articles.title: Scientists Find Plasmids That Encourage Tough Biofilms to Form
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers identified plasmid-encoded genes that promote robust biofilm formation. Genetic screening is described as revealing plasmids that enhance extracellular matrix production. Biofilms containing these plasmids were reported to show increased antibiotic resistance. The article suggests that understanding these mechanisms may help address persistent infections.

articles.title: Why Culture Still Matters in Candida Diagnostics
articles.summary_4_sentences: The article reviews why culture methods remain important for diagnosing Candida infections. It references global guidelines that describe culture as a gold-standard technique. The piece outlines common Candida species and their clinical relevance. It notes the content is sponsored by Thermo Fisher Scientific.

articles.title: Uncloaking a Sleeping Sickness Mystery
articles.summary_4_sentences: This article discusses sleeping sickness (trypanosomiasis) as a neglected tropical disease. It highlights ongoing challenges in diagnosis and treatment. The report notes the pathogen's impact on affected populations. It calls for increased attention to disease control efforts.

articles.title: Soil Microbes Help Reduce the Risk Pathogens Pose to Humans
articles.summary_4_sentences: Environmental sampling identified soil microbial communities that antagonize human pathogens. Laboratory assays showed certain soil bacteria can inhibit growth of disease-causing microbes. Metagenomic analyses linked these effects to specific antimicrobial compounds. The authors propose leveraging soil microbiota as a natural strategy to reduce pathogen exposure.

articles.title: This new test could transform UTI treatment with same-day results
articles.summary_4_sentences: The article reports a urine test that can identify effective antibiotics for UTIs in under six hours. It describes the approach as testing directly from urine rather than waiting for standard culture-based workflows. In trials involving hundreds of patient samples, the test reportedly matched standard methods in over 96% of cases. The work is presented as a way to speed treatment decisions and improve antibiotic selection.

articles.title: Identifying The Viruses We Carry & How They Affect Us
articles.summary_4_sentences: The article discusses the human virome and its potential impact on health. It describes efforts to catalog viruses commonly present in the body and evaluate their effects. The report notes that viral interactions may be benign or potentially harmful. It argues for comprehensive virome profiling to better understand these relationships.

topic_groups.topic: Mental Health, Stress, and Behavioral Neuroscience

articles.title: Scientists say 7 days of meditation can rewire your brain
articles.summary_4_sentences: The article reports that one week of intensive meditation and mind-body practices led to measurable changes across the brain and body. It describes improved brain efficiency, immune signaling changes, and increased natural pain-relief chemicals in blood. The report also states effects included neuron growth and stronger brain connectivity. It notes the observed state resembled psychedelic-like brain states without drug use.

articles.title: Snippets of hair may expose chronic stress in war refugees
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers analyzed hair samples from war refugees to assess chronic stress. The study found biochemical markers consistent with prolonged stress exposure. The findings suggest hair could serve as a noninvasive biomarker of trauma. The article indicates this could help improve mental health screening in displaced populations.

articles.title: These overlooked brain cells may control fear and PTSD
articles.summary_4_sentences: The article reports evidence that astrocytes contribute to fear memory formation, recall, and weakening. It states that astrocytes interact with neurons in real time during fear-related processes. Manipulating astrocyte activity is reported to change the strength of fear memories. The findings are presented as a possible path toward new treatments for anxiety-related disorders including PTSD.

articles.title: When our minds wander to the body, it may affect mental health
articles.summary_4_sentences: This investigation examined how attention to bodily sensations relates to mental health outcomes. Participants who focused on bodily sensations reported reduced symptoms of depression and ADHD. The effect is linked in the report to enhanced mindfulness and self-regulation. The authors suggest body-focused techniques could be incorporated into therapy.

articles.title: Depression Increases in Fathers 1 Year After Childbirth
articles.summary_4_sentences: This report states that rates of depression and stress-related disorders increase among new fathers one year after childbirth. It highlights the timing of symptom increases at the one-year mark. The summary frames the issue as relevant to postnatal mental health beyond mothers. The provided text does not describe mechanisms or specific interventions.

topic_groups.topic: Public Health, Toxins, and Environmental Exposure

articles.title: Unusual airborne toxin detected in the U.S. for the first time
articles.summary_4_sentences: Scientists searching for air-pollution clues detected toxic MCCPs in the air in the Western Hemisphere for the first time, according to the article. The report suggests a likely source is fertilizer produced from sewage sludge. It frames this as an unexpected contamination pathway. The piece highlights the detection as a new concern for tracking and controlling airborne pollutants.

articles.title: Erythritol Affects Brain Cells, May Increase Stroke Risk
articles.summary_4_sentences: In vitro experiments assessed erythritol effects on cultured brain cells and reported oxidative stress and altered mitochondrial function. The report also describes animal findings of increased markers of vascular inflammation after chronic erythritol consumption. Based on these results, the authors propose high intake of erythritol could elevate stroke risk. The article emphasizes that further investigation is warranted.

articles.title: Breakthrough water filter removes 98% of toxic PFAS forever chemicals
articles.summary_4_sentences: The article reports a new approach for trapping PFAS in water using nanosized cages that bind PFAS molecules. It states the method can capture short-chain PFAS, which are described as difficult to remove with current technologies. Tests are reported to remove up to 98% of these pollutants and remain effective after multiple uses. The work is presented as a step toward more effective water filtration systems.

articles.title: Study finds dangerous lead levels in children’s clothing
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers tested children's shirts from multiple retailers and reported that every sample exceeded U.S. safety limits for lead. The article highlights risk for young children because they may chew on clothing. It reports that bright colors such as red and yellow had particularly high lead levels, potentially due to dye-fixing chemicals. Simulations described in the report suggest brief mouthing could lead to unsafe lead exposure associated with harms to brain development and behavior.

articles.title: Scientists discover reversible male birth control that stops sperm production
articles.summary_4_sentences: The article reports a mouse study of a nonhormonal compound, JQ1, intended as reversible male contraception. It states the compound temporarily shut down meiosis, stopping sperm production. After treatment stopped, sperm production and fertility returned. The report adds that the animals produced healthy offspring after recovery.

articles.title: Artemis II ends its historic lunar journey
articles.summary_4_sentences: The article reports that NASA's Artemis II mission completed its first crewed lunar flyby. It states the spacecraft performed a successful lunar swing-by and returned to Earth safely. Data collected are described as informing future Artemis missions aimed at lunar landing. The piece frames the mission as demonstrating readiness for a sustained lunar presence.

articles.title: Even before splashdown, Artemis II is delivering a scientific treasure trove
articles.summary_4_sentences: The article reports that Artemis II collected scientific measurements while en route. Instruments recorded magnetic fields, radiation levels, and dust particle distributions. The dataset is described as providing insights relevant to spacecraft design and astronaut safety. Researchers are reported to anticipate multiple publications based on these observations.

articles.title: Exploding black holes could explain an antimatter mystery
articles.summary_4_sentences: Theoretical physicists propose that evaporating black holes could generate excess antimatter. The article reports simulations suggesting the emitted particles might match observed cosmic-ray anomalies. This is presented as a new hypothesis for explaining antimatter excess. The report emphasizes that additional observations are required to test the model.

articles.title: Just 10,000 quantum bits may crack internet encryption schemes
articles.summary_4_sentences: A cryptographic analysis estimated that a quantum computer with 10,000 qubits could break RSA encryption. The article notes required error-correction thresholds for such a device. It argues these estimates increase urgency for adopting post-quantum cryptography. The report also mentions potential mitigation strategies discussed by the authors.

articles.title: Most U.S. states are warming but not in the way you think
articles.summary_4_sentences: The article reports that U.S. warming patterns are uneven and not fully captured by average temperature trends alone. It states that only about half of states show rising average temperatures, yet most show changes such as hotter highs or warmer lows. Regional differences are described, with the West seeing more extreme heat and the North losing cold extremes. The findings are presented as showing climate change impacts vary depending on location and metric.

articles.title: Emperor penguins are marching toward extinction. Antarctica fur seals too
articles.summary_4_sentences: Conservation scientists assessed population trends for emperor penguins and Antarctic fur seals. The article reports significant declines linked to climate change and sea-ice loss. Modeling is described as predicting continued habitat reduction that could push both species toward extinction. The authors call for urgent international protection measures.

articles.title: Hawaii is turning ocean plastic into roads to fight pollution
articles.summary_4_sentences: Engineers developed a process that incorporates reclaimed ocean plastic into asphalt mixtures. Field tests are reported to show durability comparable to conventional road materials. The initiative is presented as reducing marine debris while supporting sustainable infrastructure. Hawaii is described as planning expansion of the technology to additional highways.

articles.title: New Liquid Crystal Robot Moves Without Motors or Gears
articles.summary_4_sentences: Engineers reported a soft robot made from liquid crystal elastomers that moves without traditional actuators. The material changes shape in response to temperature gradients, producing directional movement. Experiments demonstrated crawling and turning motions. The article suggests this motor-free approach could simplify manufacturing of bioinspired robotic systems.

articles.title: Mummified reptile hints at the origins of how we breathe
articles.summary_4_sentences: Paleontologists examined a fossilized reptile with preserved respiratory structures. The anatomy is described as indicating an early adaptation for efficient gas exchange. The findings are presented as bridging a gap between ancient reptiles and modern vertebrate breathing mechanisms. The article says this work reshapes theories about respiratory evolution.

articles.title: Should We Hold the Lamb at Easter Dinner?
articles.summary_4_sentences: The article discusses the tradition of serving lamb at Easter meals. It reviews historical and religious significance associated with the practice. It also notes ethical and dietary perspectives on the tradition. The piece invites readers to reflect on personal choices about whether to continue it.

articles.title: Supreme Court ruling on ‘conversion therapy’ puts medical talk in the hot seat
articles.summary_4_sentences: The article reports on a Supreme Court decision that declared conversion therapy illegal in several states. It states that legal analysts argue the ruling creates challenges for medical professionals who previously offered such services. The decision is described as emphasizing protections for LGBTQ+ individuals from harmful practices. The report suggests it may prompt revisions to medical ethics guidelines nationwide.

'Tunneling Nanotubes' are Revealed As a Player in Huntington's

Hidden antibiotics in river fish spark new food safety fears
